commands.author: clawsweeper[bot]
commands.repair_reason: No narrow automated repair is indicated; the remaining decision is maintainer acceptance of the generic routeThreadId steering semantics and proof scope.; Cleared: The diff changes in-process auto-reply and Slack routing metadata plus tests, with no dependency, workflow, secret, permission, artifact download, or code-execution surface change. (sha=323e3caa96b845ab9ac07a801c2ed2be06dd35f9)
commands.review_summary:
The PR adds routed thread ids to active reply operations, blocks active steering when the current turn targets a different known route thread, and expands Slack/auto-reply regression tests.

Reproducibility: yes. at source level. Current main keeps Slack DMs on a stable session while carrying routed thread metadata, and active steering only checks the session lane; the PR body also reports the before-fix assertion failure for the same-session/different-thread case.
commands.review_followup: No narrow automated repair is indicated; the remaining decision is maintainer acceptance of the generic routeThreadId steering semantics and proof scope.

target.title: fix(slack): keep DM thread turns out of active steering
target.body:
## Summary

- Preserve OpenClaw's existing stable Slack DM `SessionKey` contract for top-level DMs and ordinary DM thread replies.
- Track the routed thread id on every active reply operation path, including agent-runner-owned, dispatch-owned, and queued follow-up operations.
- Suppress steering when a new Slack DM turn has the same session key but a different Slack thread id, so overlapping DM threads do not append into each other's active run.

## Verification

- Confirmed latest `origin/main` at `1d1a7c26d8867fdfa98c97d8b510ad0ce1951858` still does not pass `routeThreadId` when `dispatch-from-config.ts` or `followup-runner.ts` create reply operations.
- Watched the original regression fail before the fix: `expected true to be false` for `call.shouldSteer`, proving the second Slack DM thread would still steer into the active run.
- `pnpm test src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/followup-runner.test.ts -- -t "records routed Slack thread id|records queued thread id"`
- `pnpm test extensions/slack/src/monitor/message-handler/prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts extensions/slack/src/monitor/message-handler/prepare.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply-run.media-only.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/followup-runner.test.ts`
- `pnpm check:changed`
- `git diff --check`

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: Two overlapping top-level Slack DMs from the same Slack sender should keep the stable DM `SessionKey`, but the second top-level Slack thread must not be steered into the first active run. Ordinary DM thread follow-up should continue to work in the original Slack thread.
- Real environment tested: Real Slack workspace `w1773415900-eqa641873.slack.com`, OpenClaw source checkout `323e3caa96b845ab9ac07a801c2ed2be06dd35f9`, foreground gateway started from this branch with `node scripts/run-node.mjs gateway run --port 18790 --verbose --ws-log compact`. The already-running installed gateway was `2026.5.12`, so I stopped it before the smoke and used the PR checkout gateway for the Slack event handling.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Started the PR gateway with a temporary redacted proof config using Slack Socket Mode, `replyToMode=all`, and `allowBots=true` because the available automated Slack acceptance sender posts app-authored Slack messages. The OpenClaw bot under test was a different bot id, so this did not exercise self-message handling. Sent top-level DM A and top-level DM B from the same sender in the same DM channel 0.55s apart, then sent ordinary follow-up D inside B's Slack thread.
- Evidence after fix: Redacted Slack API evidence from the live workspace:

```text
Gateway under test: OpenClaw 2026.5.24 (323e3ca)
Loaded channel runtime: Slack Socket Mode connected; octoclaw-runtime was not loaded in this proof gateway.
Workspace: w1773415900-eqa641873.slack.com
Sender: user=U...9Z bot_id=B...N2
OpenClaw bot: user=U...CQ bot_id=B...57
DM channel: D...QL
Nonce: oc-proof-mpjc9xn8

Top-level A sent: ts=1779600644.944599 thread_ts=1779600644.944599
Top-level B sent: ts=1779600645.499179 thread_ts=1779600645.499179
Delta between A and B sends: 0.55458s

A thread replies:
- user=U...9Z bot_id=B...N2 ts=1779600644.944599 thread_ts=1779600644.944599 text="[oc-proof-mpjc9xn8] top-level DM A... FINAL-A=oc-proof-mpjc9xn8-A"
- user=U...CQ bot_id=B...57 ts=1779600684.955289 thread_ts=1779600644.944599 text="... oc-proof-mpjc9xn8-A ..."

B thread replies:
- user=U...9Z bot_id=B...N2 ts=1779600645.499179 thread_ts=1779600645.499179 text="[oc-proof-mpjc9xn8] top-level DM B... FINAL-B=oc-proof-mpjc9xn8-B"
- user=U...CQ bot_id=B...57 ts=1779600690.273309 thread_ts=1779600645.499179 text="... FINAL-B=oc-proof-mpjc9xn8-B"

Ordinary DM thread follow-up in B:
- user=U...9Z bot_id=B...N2 ts=1779601018.905549 thread_ts=1779600645.499179 text="[oc-proof-mpjc9xn8] ordinary DM thread follow-up D in B thread... FINAL-D=oc-proof-mpjc9xn8-D"
- user=U...CQ bot_id=B...57 ts=1779601036.152919 thread_ts=1779600645.499179 text="Thread B continuation acknowledged ... FINAL-D=oc-proof-mpjc9xn8-D."

Local runtime transcript evidence:
- A trajectory contained stable SessionKey agent:main:slack:default:direct:u0...9z and wrote topic transcript ending in topic-1779600644.944599.jsonl.
- B trajectory contained the same stable SessionKey agent:main:slack:default:direct:u0...9z and wrote a separate topic transcript ending in topic-1779600645.499179.jsonl.
- B thread follow-up remained in Slack thread_ts=1779600645.499179 and received its own OpenClaw reply in that same thread.
```

- Observed result after fix: The two overlapping top-level DMs did not collapse into one Slack reply thread. A received an OpenClaw reply in A's Slack thread, B received an independent OpenClaw reply in B's Slack thread, and the later B-thread follow-up continued in B's thread. The local trajectories for A and B both carried the same stable DM `SessionKey` while using different routed Slack thread ids.
- What was not tested: The automated sender available in this environment posts Slack app-authored messages, so the temporary proof config enabled `allowBots=true`. This live proof still exercises the real Slack Socket Mode transport, real Slack thread ids, and the PR checkout's active-run routing boundary; it does not prove Slack's human-user token UX separately.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Before the fix, the same local runtime scenario failed with `AssertionError: expected true to be false` at `src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply-run.media-only.test.ts:1306`, showing the same-session/different-thread second turn would steer into the active run.

## Root Cause

- Root cause: Slack DMs intentionally share the stable direct-message session key, but the active-run steering decision only compared session identity. A second top-level DM from the same user could therefore steer into the active run for the first Slack message thread. The first PR revision recorded routed thread ids only for agent-runner-owned operations, leaving dispatch-owned and queued follow-up operations without the thread metadata used by the steering guard.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Existing Slack prepare tests covered the stable DM session contract, but auto-reply routing tests did not cover same-session, different-thread active runs or every operation creation path.
- Contributing context: The fix belongs at the active-run routing boundary, not in Slack session-key generation, because Slack DM session continuity is a shipped contract.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level that should have caught this: Unit test plus Slack prepare-path contract test.
- Target test or file: `src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply-run.media-only.test.ts`, `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts`, `src/auto-reply/reply/followup-runner.test.ts`, and `extensions/slack/src/monitor/message-handler/prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts`.
- Scenario the test should lock in: Same stable Slack DM session, different routed Slack thread id, active prior run, second turn must not steer into the first run; every created active reply operation should carry the routed thread id when one exists.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: It exercises the auto-reply decision that caused the overlap while separately preserving the Slack session-key contract and covering the dispatch/follow-up operation creation paths ClawSweeper flagged.
target.author: guanbear

```text
- user=U...CQ bot_id=B...57 ts=1779600684.955289 thread_ts=1779600644.944599 text="... oc-proof-mpjc9xn8-A ..."

B thread replies:
- user=U...9Z bot_id=B...N2 ts=1779600645.499179 thread_ts=1779600645.499179 text="[oc-proof-mpjc9xn8] top-level DM B... FINAL-B=oc-proof-mpjc9xn8-B"
- user=U...CQ bot_id=B...57 ts=1779600690.273309 thread_ts=1779600645.499179 text="... FINAL-B=oc-proof-mpjc9xn8-B"

Ordinary DM thread follow-up in B:
- user=U...9Z bot_id=B...N2 ts=1779601018.905549 thread_ts=1779600645.499179 text="[oc-proof-mpjc9xn8] ordinary DM thread follow-up D in B thread... FINAL-D=oc-proof-mpjc9xn8-D"
- user=U...CQ bot_id=B...57 ts=1779601036.152919 thread_ts=1779600645.499179 text="Thread B continuation acknowledged ... FINAL-D=oc-proof-mpjc9xn8-D."

Local runtime transcript evidence:
- A trajectory contained stable SessionKey agent:main:slack:default:direct:u0...9z and wrote topic transcript ending in topic-1779600644.944599.jsonl.
- B trajectory contained the same stable SessionKey agent:main:slack:default:direct:u0...9z and wrote a separate topic transcript ending in topic-1779600645.499179.jsonl.
- B thread follow-up remained in Slack thread_ts=1779600645.499179 and received its own OpenClaw reply in that same thread.
```

- Observed result after fix: The two overlapping top-level DMs did not collapse into one Slack reply thread. A received an OpenClaw reply in A's Slack thread, B received an independent OpenClaw reply in B's Slack thread, and the later B-thread follow-up continued in B's thread. The local trajectories for A and B both carried the same stable DM `SessionKey` while using different routed Slack thread ids.
- What was not tested: The automated sender available in this environment posts Slack app-authored messages, so the temporary proof config enabled `allowBots=true`. This live proof still exercises the real Slack Socket Mode transport, real Slack thread ids, and the PR checkout's active-run routing boundary; it does not prove Slack's human-user token UX separately.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Before the fix, the same local runtime scenario failed with `AssertionError: expected true to be false` at `src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply-run.media-only.test.ts:1306`, showing the same-session/different-thread second turn would steer into the active run.

## Root Cause

- Root cause: Slack DMs intentionally share the stable direct-message session key, but the active-run steering decision only compared session identity. A second top-level DM from the same user could therefore steer into the active run for the first Slack message thread. The first PR revision recorded routed thread ids only for agent-runner-owned operations, leaving dispatch-owned and queued follow-up operations without the thread metadata used by the steering guard.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Existing Slack prepare tests covered the stable DM session contract, but auto-reply routing tests did not cover same-session, different-thread active runs or every operation creation path.
- Contributing context: The fix belongs at the active-run routing boundary, not in Slack session-key generation, because Slack DM session continuity is a shipped contract.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level that should have caught this: Unit test plus Slack prepare-path contract test.
- Target test or file: `src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply-run.media-only.test.ts`, `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts`, `src/auto-reply/reply/followup-runner.test.ts`, and `extensions/slack/src/monitor/message-handler/prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts`.
- Scenario the test should lock in: Same stable Slack DM session, different routed Slack thread id, active prior run, second turn must not steer into the first run; every created active reply operation should carry the routed thread id when one exists.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: It exercises the auto-reply decision that caused the overlap while separately preserving the Slack session-key contract and covering the dispatch/follow-up operation creation paths ClawSweeper flagged.
